schools.summary: Gill: spoken while Israel was encamped at Sinai, before they journeyed thence, in the first month of the second year.
Henry: God gave particular orders for keeping this Passover, otherwise it seems they would not have kept it; it was the day twelvemonth after they came out of Egypt, before they were numbered.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the... second year
the Passover the day twelvemonth after the Exodus
the memorial of redemption renewed by God's command

schools.summary: Gill: without a fresh precept they might not have thought themselves obliged to keep it until they came into Canaan, so a new order is given.
Keil & Delitzsch: let the children of Israel keep the passover at his appointed season, the fourteenth of the first month.

themes: Let the children of Israel also keep the passover at his appointed season
a fresh command renewing the ordinance
the memorial of redemption not let lapse

schools.summary: Gill: on the fourteenth day of Nisan or Abib, the first month.
Ellicott: 'according to all the statutes and ordinances thereof,' though some directions for the Egyptian Passover could not apply in the wilderness; kept 'between the two evenings' (Exod. 12:6).

themes: In the fourteenth day... at even... according to all the rites... and... ceremonies
kept by its full ordinance
some particulars belonging to the first night alone

schools.summary: Gill: the time now drawing nigh for the observation of it, it being almost a year since their coming out of Egypt, Moses spake to the children of Israel to keep it.

themes: Moses spake unto the children of Israel, that they should keep the passover
the time drawing nigh
God's word faithfully delivered to the people

schools.summary: Gill: only the Passover is mentioned, not the seven days of unleavened bread, which was only enjoined at this time though it used to follow; perhaps the flour for so large a body could not easily be had for seven days in the wilderness.

themes: they kept the passover... in the wilderness of Sinai... according to all that the LORD commanded
the memorial kept even in the wilderness
God's commands fitted to his people's state

schools.summary: Gill: they were unclean seven days by the dead and so might not eat of any holy thing, the Passover included; they came at once to Moses, the case now provided against though not at the first institution.

themes: certain men, who were defiled by the dead body... could not keep the passover
grieved to be shut out
a longing for the ordinance, rebuking the careless

schools.summary: Gill: they put their question to Moses, the chief magistrate, the more difficult matters being brought to him.
Keil & Delitzsch: they complained that they were kept back from offering an offering of the LORD in his appointed season among the children of Israel.

themes: wherefore are we kept back, that we may not offer an offering of the LORD
grieved to be barred from the feast
hearts prizing communion with God

schools.summary: Gill: he bid them stand still where they were, at the door of the tabernacle (Aben Ezra), while he heard what the LORD would command, not deciding of himself.

themes: Stand still, and I will hear what the LORD will command concerning you
Moses waiting on God, not deciding of himself
the pattern of seeking God's mind in doubt

schools.summary: Gill: God spoke from between the cherubim, after Moses had laid the case before him.
Keil & Delitzsch: the LORD gave general instructions for everyone defiled by a corpse or on a distant journey.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
answering from the mercy seat
one need turned into a perpetual provision

schools.summary: Gill: the answer concerned not the men only but all the people, a rule for like cases in all succeeding ages.
Keil & Delitzsch: if any man of them or their posterity were unclean by a dead body, or on a distant journey, he should yet keep the passover unto the LORD.

themes: If any man... shall be unclean... or be in a journey afar off, yet he shall keep the passover
a second opportunity for the hindered
God making a way for the willing

schools.summary: Gill: the month Ijar, allowing a month for the defiled to cleanse and the absent to return; kept on the same day and hour as the first, still to keep in mind the saving of the firstborn and the deliverance from Egypt, with unleavened bread and bitter herbs.
Keil & Delitzsch: the fourteenth of the second month at even they were to keep it, with unleavened bread and bitter herbs.

themes: The fourteenth day of the second month at even they shall keep it... with unleavened bread and bitter herbs
the same day and hour as the first
the full ordinance, not a half-feast

schools.summary: Gill: none of the flesh was to be left to the morning, what remained burned with fire.
Ellicott: 'according to all the ordinance,' the primary reference being the law of the Paschal lamb, no bone of it broken.

themes: They shall leave none of it unto the morning, nor break any bone of it
kept by all the ordinances of the passover
the unbroken lamb foreshadowing Christ (John 19:36)

schools.summary: Gill: this is the man free from any pollution and not on a journey, who has no excuse for forbearing to keep the passover.
Ellicott: 'shall bear his sin' is, shall be put to death (cf. Lev. 24:15; Num. 18:22).

themes: the man that is clean... and forbeareth to keep the passover... shall be cut off
grace for the hindered, judgment for the careless
the despiser of the ordinance bears his sin

schools.summary: Gill: the stranger must first become a proselyte of righteousness and be circumcised, or he might not eat of the passover.
Ellicott: the law of the stranger — one ordinance both for the stranger and for him born in the land.

themes: if a stranger shall sojourn... and will keep the passover... ye shall have one ordinance
the believing stranger a fellow-partaker
a foreshadow of the gospel's breadth

schools.summary: Gill: on the first day of the first month of the second year, when the tabernacle was reared up, the cloud covered it.

Henry: the cloud was the visible sign and symbol of God's presence with Israel; when the tabernacle was finished it settled upon it and covered it, to show God manifested his presence there — by day a cloud, by night a fire.

themes: the cloud covered the tabernacle... at even there was... the appearance of fire
the sign of God's presence
both shade by day and light by night

schools.summary: Gill: it was so night and day, as long as Israel was in the wilderness.
Keil & Delitzsch: the covering of the dwelling with the cloud that shone by night as fire was constant, not a phenomenon that appeared only at first and then vanished.

themes: So it was alway: the cloud covered it by day, and... fire by night
the presence never withdrawn
God's abiding presence through the wilderness

schools.summary: Gill: the cloud went up from the tabernacle, the LORD himself doing it, the sign for Israel to move.
Keil & Delitzsch: as the cloud rose from the tent they broke up, and in the place where it abode they pitched their tents.

themes: when the cloud was taken up... the children of Israel journeyed: and... where the cloud abode... pitched
led by God's presence, not self-directed
following the LORD's leading

schools.summary: Gill: this was the 'mouth of the LORD' — not an audible voice, but the removal of the cloud was interpretatively his command to move.
Keil & Delitzsch: as Jehovah was with his people in the cloud, its rising and falling was his order to journey or to rest.

themes: At the commandment of the LORD the children of Israel journeyed... and... pitched
the cloud the mouth of the LORD
providence taken as God's direction

schools.summary: Gill: the cloud sometimes tarried many days, even years, in some places.
Keil & Delitzsch: whether it rested many days or few, the children of Israel kept the charge of the LORD and journeyed not.

themes: when the cloud tarried long... the children of Israel kept the charge of the LORD, and journeyed not
staying at the LORD's appointment
the harder grace of waiting

schools.summary: Gill: the 'few days' were so few they might easily be numbered, the Targum reading the seven days of a week.
Keil & Delitzsch: the same rule for the short stay as the long — at the commandment of the LORD they abode and journeyed.

themes: when the cloud was a few days... according to the commandment of the LORD they abode... and... journeyed
one rule for the short halt and the long
submission rendered equally

schools.summary: Gill: the cloud sometimes abode but a single night, rising in the morning.
Clarke: night marches were rare and hard in that desert, yet they went when the cloud led, by day or by night.
Ellicott: there must have been sentinels watching by night as by day, whose office was to give the signal to march when the cloud rose.

themes: whether it was by day or by night that the cloud was taken up, they journeyed
ready to move at any hour
instant obedience to God's signal

schools.summary: Gill: the varying stays — half a day, a day, two days, a month, even a year or longer (Maimonides says eighteen years in one place).
Clarke: it was by the divine counsel alone that they were directed in all their peregrinations.

themes: whether it were two days, or a month, or a year, that the cloud tarried
Israel stayed till it rose
continual dependence on God's leading

schools.summary: Gill: whether the stay or march were agreeable or disagreeable, they were obedient to the divine will; though an obstinate and perverse people who must have longed to reach the land, in this they rested and journeyed at the commandment of the LORD by the hand of Moses, keeping his charge.

themes: At the commandment of the LORD they rested... and... journeyed: they kept the charge of the LORD
obedient whether it pleased them or not
taking the haltings and journeys of life from God's hand
